Q_Sea: ^At the end of a long line of divine tyrants and feudal lords.
^Feels like being at the end of history itself, taking a dive off into a chasm of hope.

c-0: ^Yeah, yeah. Say that as much as you want, you're still living in a Freestrider world.
c-1: ^Obviously. You've already been obsessed with it for years at this point.
c-2: ^You may repeat that as much as you want, but the Freestrider slogans carved into the back of your skull prove otherwise. Like, purely bodily speaking.
c-3: ^A bunch of stuff. Personal freedom. The right to climb the ladder.
^Free trade is a part of it. Letting people, and the companies they own, spread their economic roots without big government stomping on them.
^Then you've got democracy, which is like, the most liberal thing out of them all.
^Mainly because it allows people to actually pick and choose their leadership, instead of it being determined by gods, spells, and steel. As is the historical precedent.

g-0: ^Not that your thoughts have any real impact on the city.
^Unless you get back to writing, of course.

s: ^So we're turning Freestrider no matter what?
^True. Their power is just too overwhelming.
^Writing? Why would I ever perform such a degenerate act?

c-5: ^And where does that power come from?

c-2: ^Exactly. They're on the right side of history!
g-0: ^Okay, I think you need to rethink your whole mindset here.

c-6: ^Look. You've got some bruises on your fingers from last time. But really, that's just proof that you were on the right trail!

afterIntro: ^Freestriderism is the future. That much is clear.
^What you saw happened in that fancy room, between Viira and her glowing guest?
^That's going to be the main conflict going forward. Not some scuffle with Azgal youth students, or rigid theocratic bureaucrats grasping to hold on to their mansions...
^As Freestriderism creeps across the Coast, there will be a lot of powers ready to meet it. Both head-on, but also in more sinister ways...
^As shown, by the Golden Horde. They're clearly trying to co-opt the whole thing.

s: ^I liked Ambassador Ivcx. Seemed like a nice fellow.
^The Golden Horde is a scourge upon the Coast. They should not be affiliated with Freestriderism at all.
^We all need to make concessions to get our policies enacted.
^Viira is doing the best she can.

c-8: ^Look, in an ideal world, yeah. That'd be swell.
c-9: ^It's all about being smart. Pick your battles. Like the purple one says: be smooth, or whatever.

g-1: ^Reeds is in a precarious position, believe it or not. It'll be all too easy for the liberal, wondrous dream to collapse in on itself.
^With enemies on all sides, within and without, you need to band together with your fellow Freestriders and FIGHT BACK.
^Or, if that doesn't work, grab what you can while the grabbin's good. Then jump out the window in a blaze of glory.

s: ^Wait, so... am I doing this for me, or for the liberal ideal?
^Hells yeah. (Feel ready to grab, and grab again.)
^That does sound like it could collapse at any second.

c-11: ^One and the same, buddy. One and the same.
c-12: ^Just like in the good old days!
c-13: ^And somehow, over these past fifty-something years of crazy, radical Freestriderism, that just hasn't happened.

g-2: ^You remember how Freestriderism was born, right?

s: ^Let's go over it again. (Listen to the political exposition.)

g-3: ^It all goes back to the Arcanists. As everything tends to do.

^Most of the their states were essentially built upon aristocratic structures. Feudal states, turned magocratic. Wizards need esoteric educations, right? Who can afford such an education? A nobleman, that's who.
^It's all economics. All the way to the top.

s: ^All the biggest Arcanist houses were all former nobles after all.

c-16: ^Of course not. But it can only be cultivated via learning.

g-4: ^So they spent the early Arcane Era building up their power. House Prax. House Hagginshags. House Diminorii. All led by men descending from long lines of royals. We've even got a theocracy, with The Shadow Lord, turning into another magocracy in the end.
^But with all that magical might, despite reaching the pinnacle of esoteric knowledge towards the second century of their era...
g-5: ^Don't get me wrong. Magic is real neat. Cutting mountains in half, flipping them over, building cities on them? Awesome. Infinite food? Convenient!
^But you can only get so far with a top-down structure.
g-6: ^Imagine you've got twenty people working under you. You assign them tasks- to, I don't know, transport your magically created bread to a thousand hungry mouths around the city.
^You, alone, could probably organize that. Twenty people ain't that much.
^But what if it wasn't twenty people feeding a thousand people- what if it was a five thousand people, making sure a hundred thousand people got fed their daily piece of fake bread?

s: ^I'd still be able to do that.

c-21: ^Yeah, yeah, brag to someone outside your own damn head.
c-22: ^The average Arcane Era city had a hell of a lot more.
c-23: ^That's two words. And yeah, you're right.

g-7: ^The point is: even the smartest, most glorious super-genius wizard-god-king will eventually be spread thin. With the crazy scale of modern society - and especially with infinite food you can bet your ass that people start breeding like giant rabbits - the Arcanists just couldn't keep up.
^Divine Era kingdoms never reached those levels, even with walking gods at the helms. So the Arcanists did what they had to.
^The old aristocrats still held most of the power, but they got dragged kicking and screaming into a world where anyone could become a wizard.
^And more importantly, those nobodies could also become stinking rich.
^That's how you get the necropolis manufacturies. The ocean trade network. Basically the entire built-in-a-factory way of life we enjoy today.

s: ^Did the Industrial Revolution begin in Old Diminor?
^But wouldn't that just lead to magocracy, led by those with money?
^Infinite food sounds lovely. We should go back to that.
^Go on. (Continue listening to the story of Freestriderism.)

c-24: ^Nah. Technically, there's been mechanical industries on Jor since the Gate War. That's how the orcs managed to join in against eldritch aberrations and divine beings.
^But our modern revolution sprouted when those same orcs rediscovered their roots.
^Bloodcity Bay used to be a scattered collection of states, but in the Divine Era the warlords turned it into the thing we know today.
c-25: ^Sure, but as long as money flows towards merit, then you'll have a fair game going.
c-26: ^You'd think that. But here's the deal: the second we entered into somewhat modernized agriculture, food scarcity stopped being a production issue.
^It became, and remains, entirely an issue of logistics. We've got so much damn food being made, and the only problem is getting it into the mouths of the people.

^Magical food then becomes like rotten waterfall: if bread is free, then the people growing the wheat can't get paid as much. Thus eventually they'll stop growing it. Who gets most impacted when food production disappears from an economy? The working class. On top of that, if the food supply becomes entirely centralized with spellcasters, then food production gets inverted from the natural method - instead of food being created rurally and moved into urban areas, instead food has to be moved from cities to smaller communities.

^And once again, you'll also get the problem of top-down governing. By centralizing food production into a handful of extremely powerful wizards (or maybe a few thousand, at the peak of certain city-states) then even the most minor mistake can cause widespread famines.
^Which is why every single city-state that attempted this 'free food' method failed.
^And any that try it in the future will fail too.
^Large enough systems require decentralization and economic liberalism to even function. At least in the long term.

^Liberalism is thus inevitable in a modern world. Even the most illiberal states today use parts of Freestrider theorem to rule. Even esoteric autocracies like the Golden Horde or oligarchies like Bloodcity Bay.
^You'll bet your ass they're all hopping on the free-trade wagon. Even if they're desperate to hold onto their outdated regimes.

g-8: ^This was a period when national borders didn't really exist yet. It was more of... pseudo feudal thing going on. Royalty owning land, and the serfs on it. Not so much 'folk belonging to nationstates' as we see it today.

^But, those early magocrats still wanted centralized control, so they pushed to drive everyone into their cities. In that century of industrialization, literally millions of humans moved from scattered rural towns into one of the capitals of Old Diminor.
^Hells, they had humans from all around the Coast moving - we're talking entire civilization uprooted - just to go live in the modern world of the Arcanists.
^To work in factories. To have cheap glowstones and running water. To live in peace, yes, but also to have actual personal freedoms.

^People could move between city-states, for the most part. A man could rise above his station and make something out of himself- even if the top gigs were all held by those in power. There's a reason we don't call it mago-feudalism, or whatever. Most Arcanist states actually had a fair bit of liberalism growing.
^Even today we still live and die by the city-state format the Arcanists set in stone.
g-9: ^Obviously this is an abridged version, but when the Coast War broke out at the end of the 300s...
^Skip forward a few decades. Suddenly you have to have documentation wherever you go.
^Citizenship became a curse or a blessing, depending on which one you had- and when.
^Like, most people just think about the Coast War as this huge apocalyptic event where ninety percent of humans died.
^But that didn't really happen until the real fighting in the 470s.
^It was six decades of paranoia, escalating tensions, local conflicts, proxy wars, and mago-industrial madness.
^The market didn't go to war. The invisible hand of the market would have preferred to live in peace.

s: ^I'm sure the war was very profitable for those industrialists.
^Free Trade. The Freestriders made that declaration as a response to this period of isolationism, I assume.

c-31: ^Sure, lots of money to be made crafting war machines, armor, weapons.
^But what happens when you run out of poor fools to wear them?
^What happens when the factories go empty?
^And even worse: what happens when those powerful Arcanists begin seizing the means of productions to fuel their war machines even further?
^There's no profit to be found when Mister Wizard comes to take your factory.

g-10: ^Slowly at first, and then quickly, the Arcanists stopped everyone from moving about.
^The cities that focused on necromancy even ran out of bodies to animate. Could you imagine?
^The entire system that let these fucking Arcanists sit on their thrones - and the hard working capitalists that helped them build it - all of it, gone.
^Some people say it was 'weakening magic' that threw down those idiots.
^Ask the people who saw it in Reeds, the ones who survived, and they'll tell you...
^The Arcanists lost because they assumed they alone were responsible for the greatness of Old Diminor.
^When in reality, it was all capitalism, baby!
g-11: ^Yeah. In 481 AE you got the Fall of Thazen, as the final nail in the almost figurative coffin.
^Within a year, the ruins were covered in gold-yellow flags.
^Those heroic and oppressed industrialists had rallied the people to fight for universal suffrage.

s: ^They exchanged one set of corrupt leaders for another.

c-36: ^Yeah, yeah. You've read all the Azgalist propaganda as well.
c-37: ^Comparing capitalists to Arcanists is like comparing a hot stove to a Fireball.
^One can be mishandled, sure. The other will leave you with third degree burns all over your body. It's like, not even comparable.
^Just cook a nice meal and shut up!
c-38: ^You did spend a year or so being obsessed with Reedsian literature on the topic.

expositionSkip: ^Point is, the Invisible Hand of the Market is guiding us. And it'll keep guiding us towards a world that's a hell of a lot more liberal and a hell of a lot more prosperous.
^Just as long as you avoid falling into some Azgalist propaganda hole again.
^This tale you have heard so many times.
^A modern myth. As the old keep being stripped away.
^Freestriderism. An Unstoppable Force, charging across the Coast.
^You must arm yourself against the storm. Quickly. Before you are crushed between the waves.
^There are a hundred different ideologies with a thousand different interpretations and branches.
^Out of all of them? Reedsian Freestriderism is probably the most powerful at the moment.
^You shouldn't throw all your eggs in the proverbial bag of holding, but it certainly won't hurt to move a bit closer underneath that yellow banner.

^So if you've got all of these forces pushing you in all kinds of directions- maybe try being a bit of an Immovable Object? One that's sitting squarely in the good graces of your new Reedsian overlords...
^Seriously. If even the apolitical boogeyman can agree to lean Freestrider, you really can't go wrong, can you?

s: ^Let's pick one of these. (Choose a feat.)

c-39: ^Bear arms. Fight against the forces of the world.
^Unstoppable Force - Alters ability score modifiers on WEAPONS. Positive modifiers get +1 and negative modifiers get -1.
c-40: ^You just gotta reach out and... grab!
^Invisible Hand - Advantage on all trifle checks.
c-41: ^A convenient cooperation. With no real downsides to it.
^Let those Freestrider impulses guide your purchasing decisions...
^Immovable Object - Your Dexterity modifier also adds to your haggle discount in shops, stacking with Charisma.

g-12: ^Remember to think like a capitalist. Which means, choose whatever works best for you.

c-44: ^Soon enough, you'll get that sweet, sweet ownership over everything you ever wanted.
c-45: ^This will be a highly productive collaboration.
^Oh yes. As long as you stop it with that fence-sitting bullshit. Now go! Buy low, sell high!
